title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
Preventing Nerve Damage with Better Diabetes Care
Diabetes is a common long-term condition that needs steady care. When it isn’t managed well, it can silently harm different parts of the body, including the kidneys, eyes, heart, and especially the nerves. This nerve damage, known as diabetic neuropathy, occurs when blood sugar levels remain high for an extended period because the body either doesn’t produce enough insulin or the cells stop responding to it. The International Diabetes Federation Atlas data from 2019 shows that 1 in 3 people with diabetes develop neuropathy in India. In this blog, we’ll break down what neuropathy really is, the signs to watch for, and how better diabetes care can help prevent long-term nerve damage.
What Is Diabetic Neuropathy?
Diabetes often develops due to insulin resistance, and factors like genetics, unhealthy eating habits, lack of exercise, obesity, and high blood pressure all increase that risk. One serious complication of long-standing diabetes is diabetic neuropathy, a condition where the nerves are damaged because of poor blood flow and consistently high sugar levels.
When blood sugar levels remain uncontrolled, the nerves gradually lose their ability to function properly. They stop sending clear signals to various body parts, which can lead to
Numbness
Tingling
burning sensations or pain
Reduced muscle strength
Difficulty in maintaining balance and stability.
Over time, these issues can interfere with simple everyday tasks and affect the overall quality of life.
Why Nerve Damage Happens in Diabetes?
Nerve damage develops slowly, and high blood sugar is the primary culprit. When glucose stays elevated for long periods, it harms the tiny blood vessels that supply your nerves. Without sufficient oxygen and nutrients, nerves begin to malfunction, leading to pain, numbness, or weakness. Several factors can raise this risk:
Long-standing diabetes: The more years you live with uncontrolled blood sugar, the more stress your nerves endure.
Kidney problems: When the kidneys struggle, it soon has a side effect on the nerve functionality.
Excess body weight: Carrying too much weight puts pressure on nerves and affects blood flow.
Smoking: It restricts blood circulation, which gradually causes nerve injury.
Slowed metabolism: In diabetes, the body’s metabolic process becomes less efficient, making it harder for nerves to get nutrients and clear waste.
Toxic build-up: Constant high sugar creates damaging substances in the blood, which slowly wear down and injure nerve cells.
How to Prevent Nerve Damage in Diabetes?
Here are a few steady lifestyle choices to protect your nerves by regulating your blood sugar, blood pressure, and overall health:
Keep BP in range: Try to stay below 140/90 mm Hg to reduce strain on your nerves.
Limit alcohol and quit smoking: Both can make nerve damage worse, so cutting back helps protect your nerves long term.
Regulate blood sugar: The most obvious solution is maintaining an ideal glucose level at all times in the body through proper medication and a balanced diet.
Eat balanced meals: Include food rich in antioxidants, omega-3 fatty acids, and vitamins such as whole grains, colourful fruits and veggies, lean protein, and nuts to support nerve function.
Stay active: Aim for regular movement, about 150 minutes a week, to boost blood flow and stabilise sugar levels.
Get routine check-ups: Regular doctor visits help track changes early and adjust treatment before problems grow.
Manage stress: Practices like yoga, meditation, or simple deep breathing can ease nerve pain and improve your overall mood.
Care for your feet: Check them daily, wear good footwear, and keep the skin moisturised. Never walk barefoot, as minor cuts can be harmful in the long run.
Check sensation: Keep monitoring the feelings in your hands and legs. If you notice any numbness or tingling sensations, inform your doctor immediately.
Treatment Options for Nerve Damage
If you already have diabetic neuropathy, here are some simple ways in which doctors can help manage nerve pain and improve daily comfort:
Medications like pain relievers, certain antidepressants, and anticonvulsants can ease nerve pain and improve sleep.
Topical options such as capsaicin creams or lidocaine patches offer relief right where it hurts.
Physical therapy helps improve balance, strength, and movement through guided exercises.
Transcutaneous Electrical Nerve Stimulation (TENS) therapy uses mild electrical pulses to reduce pain signals.
Acupuncture may help relax the body and reduce nerve-related discomfort.
Massage therapy boosts circulation and eases muscle tension, offering gentle relief.
Protect Nerves with Daily Care
Managing diabetes isn’t always easy, but protecting your nerves is possible when you stay consistent with the basics: maintaining steady glucose levels, engaging in regular physical activity, mindful eating, taking proper care of your feet, and undertaking regular check-ups. With the right care, you can slow nerve damage, feel more in control of your health, and keep doing the things you love without constant discomfort.
Key Takeaways
Diabetic neuropathy develops slowly, often due to long-term high sugar levels and poor blood flow to the nerves.
Pay attention to early signs like tingling, numbness, or burning pain.
Obesity, kidney issues, smoking, and slow metabolism are some factors that increase the chances of nerve damage.
Healthy habits such as monitoring blood sugar and BP, eating balanced meals, exercising regularly, and managing stress make a big difference.
Good foot care is essential because nerve damage can make injuries go unnoticed.
Regular check-ups and timely treatment options, such as medications, physical therapy, or holistic therapies, help manage symptoms and prevent complications.
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)
What is diabetic neuropathy?Diabetic neuropathy is nerve damage caused by long-term high blood sugar. It affects how your nerves carry signals to body parts, often leading to numbness, tingling, or pain, mostly in the hands and feet.
Can nerve damage from diabetes lead to leg amputation?Yes, severe nerve damage can increase the risk. When you can’t feel injuries or cuts in your feet, they may worsen without you noticing. If the infection spreads, it may lead to amputation. That’s why proper blood sugar control is important.
How can someone manage or reduce nerve damage?Good blood sugar control, regular exercise, a balanced diet, and proper foot care go a long way. Managing stress and avoiding smoking or excess alcohol also helps protect the nerves.
When should you see a doctor for nerve symptoms?If you notice persistent numbness, burning, sharp pain, or sores that aren’t healing, it’s best to visit a doctor early. Timely medical intervention can prevent the problem from worsening.
Are there treatments that help with diabetic nerve pain?Yes. Doctors may prescribe necessary medications for pain relief. Physical therapy or alternative methods, such as acupuncture or TENS, may also reduce pain.
Disclaimer
The content of this article is intended for informational purposes only and does not provide any professional medical/nutritional advice. Kindly consult your Dietician/Nutritionist/Doctor for anyassistance/advice. full_pdf_text:
Preventing Nerve Damage with Better Diabetes Care
Diabetes is a common long-term condition that needs steady care. When it isn’t managed well, it can silently harm different parts of the body, including the kidneys, eyes, heart, and especially the nerves. This nerve damage, known as diabetic neuropathy, occurs when blood sugar levels remain high for an extended period because the body either doesn’t produce enough insulin or the cells stop responding to it. The International Diabetes Federation Atlas data from 2019 shows that 1 in 3 people with diabetes develop neuropathy in India.1 In this blog, we’ll break down what neuropathy really is, the signs to watch for, and how better diabetes care can help prevent long-term nerve damage.
What Is Diabetic Neuropathy?
Diabetes often develops due to insulin resistance, and factors like genetics, unhealthy eating habits, lack of exercise, obesity, and high blood pressure all increase that risk. One serious complication of long-standing diabetes is diabetic neuropathy, a condition where the nerves are damaged because of poor blood flow and consistently high sugar levels.
When blood sugar levels remain uncontrolled, the nerves gradually lose their ability to function properly. They stop sending clear signals to various body parts, which can lead to
● Numbness
● Tingling
● burning sensations or pain
● Reduced muscle strength
● Difficulty in maintaining balance and stability.
Over time, these issues can interfere with simple everyday tasks and affect the overall quality of life.
Why Nerve Damage Happens in Diabetes?
Nerve damage develops slowly, and high blood sugar is the primary culprit. When glucose stays elevated for long periods, it harms the tiny blood vessels that supply your nerves. Without sufficient oxygen and nutrients, nerves begin to malfunction, leading to pain, numbness, or weakness. Several factors can raise this risk:
● Long-standing diabetes: The more years you live with uncontrolled blood sugar, the more stress your nerves endure.
● Kidney problems: Uncontrolled diabetes damage the blood vessels in the kidneys. This damage can lead to diabetic nephropathy which also increases nerve damage.
● Excess body weight: Obesity, coupled with diabetes, tend to injure the peripheral and central nerves.
● Smoking: Smoking can interfere in the normal functioning of cells, cause inflammation, and reduce the effectiveness of insulin making you prone to diabetes. Once diabetes sets in, and is left unchecked, your nerves get stressed.
● Slowed metabolism: In diabetes, the body’s metabolic process becomes less efficient, making it harder for nerves to get nutrients and clear waste.
● Toxic build-up: Constant high sugar creates damaging substances in the blood, which slowly wear down and injure nerve cells.
How to Prevent Nerve Damage in Diabetes?
Here are a few steady lifestyle choices to protect your nerves by regulating your blood sugar, blood pressure, and overall health:
● Keep BP in range: Try to stay below 140/90 mm Hg to reduce strain on your nerves.1
● Limit alcohol and quit smoking: Both can make nerve damage worse, so cutting back helps protect your nerves long term.1
● Regulate blood sugar: The most obvious solution is maintaining an ideal glucose level at all times in the body through proper medication and a balanced diet.
● Eat balanced meals: Include food rich in antioxidants, omega-3 fatty acids, and vitamins such as whole grains, colourful fruits and veggies, lean protein, and nuts to support nerve function.
● Stay active: Aim for regular movement, about 150 minutes a week, to boost blood flow and stabilise sugar levels.
● Get routine check-ups: Regular doctor visits help track changes early and adjust treatment before problems grow.
● Manage stress: Practices like yoga, meditation, or simple deep breathing can ease nerve pain and improve your overall mood.
● Care for your feet: Check them daily, wear good footwear, and keep the skin moisturised. Never walk barefoot, as minor cuts can be harmful in the long run.
● Check sensation: Keep monitoring the feelings in your hands and legs. If you notice any numbness or tingling sensations, inform your doctor immediately.
Treatment Options for Nerve Damage
If you already have diabetic neuropathy, here are some simple ways in which doctors can help manage nerve pain and improve daily comfort:2
● Medications like pain relievers, certain antidepressants, and anticonvulsants can ease nerve pain and improve sleep.
● Topical options such as capsaicin creams or lidocaine patches offer relief right where it hurts.
● Physical therapy helps improve balance, strength, and movement through guided exercises.
● Transcutaneous Electrical Nerve Stimulation (TENS) therapy uses mild electrical pulses to reduce pain signals.
● Acupuncture may help relax the body and reduce nerve-related discomfort.
● Massage therapy boosts circulation and eases muscle tension, offering gentle relief.
Protect Nerves with Daily Care
Managing diabetes isn’t always easy, but protecting your nerves is possible when you stay consistent with the basics: maintaining steady glucose levels, engaging in regular physical activity, mindful
eating, taking proper care of your feet, and undertaking regular check-ups. With the right care, you can slow nerve damage, feel more in control of your health, and keep doing the things you love without constant discomfort.
Key Takeaways
● Diabetic neuropathy develops slowly, often due to long-term high sugar levels and poor blood flow to the nerves.
● Pay attention to early signs like tingling, numbness, or burning pain.
● Obesity, kidney issues, smoking, and slow metabolism are some factors that increase the chances of nerve damage.
● Healthy habits such as monitoring blood sugar and BP, eating balanced meals, exercising regularly, and managing stress make a big difference.
● Good foot care is essential because nerve damage can make injuries go unnoticed.
● Regular check-ups and timely treatment options, such as medications, physical therapy, or holistic therapies, help manage symptoms and prevent complications.
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)
1. What is diabetic neuropathy?
Diabetic neuropathy is nerve damage caused by long-term high blood sugar. It affects how your nerves carry signals to body parts, often leading to numbness, tingling, or pain, mostly in the hands and feet. 2. Can nerve damage from diabetes lead to leg amputation?
Yes, severe nerve damage can increase the risk. When you can’t feel injuries or cuts in your feet, they may worsen without you noticing. If the infection spreads, it may lead to amputation. That’s why proper blood sugar control is important. 3. How can someone manage or reduce nerve damage?
Good blood sugar control, regular exercise, a balanced diet, and proper foot care go a long way. Managing stress and avoiding smoking or excess alcohol also helps protect the nerves. 4. When should you see a doctor for nerve symptoms?
If you notice persistent numbness, burning, sharp pain, or sores that aren’t healing, it’s best to visit a doctor early. Timely medical intervention can prevent the problem from worsening. 5. Are there treatments that help with diabetic nerve pain?
Yes. Doctors may prescribe necessary medications for pain relief. Physical therapy or alternative methods, such as acupuncture or TENS, may also reduce pain.
Disclaimer
The content of this article is intended for informational purposes only and does not provide any professional medical/nutritional advice. Kindly consult your Dietician/Nutritionist/Doctor for any assistance/advice. Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) disclaims any warranty, responsibility or liability for the accuracy of the above information and assumes no responsibility for errors or omissions.
